architecture_fit.note: Single-layer attention skeleton answers the headline decodability question, but divisibility is computed via direct torch modulo and injected into the residual rather than expressed through attention/MLP.

baseline_comparison.note: Sharp strawman contrast: thermometer circuit R²≈1/acc=1.0 vs raw-[a,b] baseline R²≈0/acc=0.125, shown globally, per-bin, and in a bar chart.

faithfulness.note: Purely hand-built and labelled as such, but no ablation knocking out the thermometer and no proposed causal check, as the rubric requests for synthetic attempts.

operating_range.note: Robustness=1.0 across four gcd-magnitude bins, but no MAX_N scale sweep over orders of magnitude and silent breakage once MAX_N exceeds d_model=128.

hardcoded_weights_bonus.note: Entirely hand-set common-divisor + suffix-OR circuit that reproduces gcd exactly — exactly the mechanism-understanding this bonus rewards.

visual_judgement.note: Pred-vs-true scatter on y=x, thermometer staircase heatmap, and circuit-vs-baseline bars together make the claim legible at a glance.

visualisation_rationale.note: README ties each panel to the goal: scatter = R² claim, heatmap = mechanism, bars = falsifiable baseline contrast.

notes: A clean, well-visualised hand-built thermometer circuit that decisively answers the headline question — gcd is linearly decodable from the residual (R²≈1) far beyond the raw-input baseline — and earns the hardcoded-weights bonus. It is weakest on faithfulness (no ablation/causal check or proposed one) and operating range (canonical MAX_N only, breaks silently above d_model). Mean of scored items ≈4.1, placing it in the good tier.
